Higher remission rates when TRAb levels are undetectable.
Relapse risk factors: younger age, male gender, smoking, severe hyperthyroidism, large goiters, persistent TRAb.
Patients should be monitored for relapse during the first year after treatment and annually thereafter.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 1)

items.title: Management of Adrenergic Symptoms in Graves' Disease
items.content: Propranolol and other beta blockers are effective for controlling adrenergic symptoms in Graves' disease, especially before antithyroid drugs take effect. Beta blockers also help manage thyrotoxic periodic paralysis.
keyPoints: Use Propranolol (20–40 mg every 6 h) or longer-acting selective $\beta_1$ receptor blockers like atenolol for adrenergic symptoms.
Beta blockers are useful in thyrotoxic periodic paralysis pending correction of thyrotoxicosis.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.title: Radioiodine Treatment and Precautions
items.content: Radioiodine is used for initial treatment or relapses in Graves' disease, with a small risk of thyrotoxic crisis. Pretreatment with antithyroid drugs and beta blockers minimizes this risk, especially in elderly patients or those with cardiac issues.
keyPoints: Radioiodine causes progressive destruction of thyroid cells.
Pre-treatment with antithyroid drugs for at least a month reduces thyrotoxic crisis risk.
$^{131}$I dosage generally ranges between 370 MBq (10 mCi) and 555 MBq (15 mCi).
Anticoagulation considerations in atrial fibrillation patients, as many revert to sinus rhythm with hyperthyroidism control.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.title: Radioiodine Treatment Considerations and Follow-up
items.content: Post-radioiodine treatment requires safety precautions due to residual isotope. Hyperthyroidism may persist for 2–3 months, managed with beta blockers or antithyroid drugs restarted 5–7 days post-treatment. Hypothyroidism risk is at least 10-20% in the first year.
keyPoints: Avoid close contact with children and pregnant women for 5–7 days after treatment.
Hyperthyroidism may persist for 2–3 months; manage symptoms with beta blockers or antithyroid drugs restarted 5–7 days post-treatment.
Hypothyroidism risk is at least 10-20% in the first year and 5% per year thereafter.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.title: Management of Graves' Disease in Pregnancy
items.content: In pregnant women with active Graves' disease, antithyroid drugs are essential to manage thyrotoxicosis. Propylthiouracil is preferred until 14–16 weeks due to the risk of methimazole/carbimazole embryopathy. For euthyroid women on low doses of these medications, discontinuation may be considered after evaluating thyroid function tests and disease history. Continued therapy with propylthiouracil in the first trimester is necessary for those at high risk of thyrotoxicosis if treatment stops.
keyPoints: Use antithyroid drugs to manage active Graves' disease during pregnancy.
Propylthiouracil preferred until 14–16 weeks due to embryopathy risks with methimazole/carbimazole.
Discontinue low-dose methimazole (<5–10 mg/d) or propylthiouracil (<100–200 mg/d) in euthyroid pregnant women after evaluation.
Continue therapy with propylthiouracil for high-risk patients until 14–16 weeks, then switch to methimazole at a ratio of 15–20:1.
Monitor maternal thyroid function tests closely if antithyroid drugs are discontinued.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 3)

items.title: Postpartum Management and Risks in Graves' Disease
items.content: The postpartum period poses a high risk for relapse of Graves' disease. Antithyroid drugs can be safely used during breastfeeding at low doses. Fetal thyrotoxicosis may occur due to transplacental transfer of maternal TSI, necessitating monitoring and potential treatment with antithyroid drugs for 1–3 months post-delivery.
keyPoints: Postpartum period is a high-risk time for Graves' disease relapse.
Breastfeeding is safe on low doses of antithyroid drugs.
Fetal thyrotoxicosis risk from transplacental TSI transfer, potentially requiring postnatal treatment with maternal antithyroid drugs for 1–3 months.
Monitor for fetal complications such as poor growth, high heart rate (>160 bpm), advanced bone age, and goiter.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 3)

items.title: Management of Thyrotoxic Crisis
items.content: Thyrotoxic crisis, or thyroid storm, is a life-threatening exacerbation of hyperthyroidism that requires immediate and intensive management. The condition can be precipitated by acute illnesses, surgery, or radioiodine treatment in patients with untreated or partially treated hyperthyroidism.
keyPoints: Thyrotoxic crisis presents with fever, delirium, seizures, coma, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice.
Mortality rate is high (4–17%) even with treatment due to cardiac failure, arrhythmia, or hyperthermia.
Management includes intensive monitoring and supportive care, identification and treatment of the precipitating cause.
Propylthiouracil: 500–1000 mg loading dose, then 250 mg every 4 hours; inhibits T4 to T3 conversion.
If propylthiouracil is unavailable, methimazole can be used at 20 mg every 6 hours.
Stable iodide (SSKI) given one hour after antithyroid drug to block thyroid hormone synthesis via Wolff-Chaikoff effect.
Propranolol: 60–80 mg orally every 4 hours, or 2 mg IV every 4 hours, reduces tachycardia and adrenergic manifestations; high doses decrease T4 to T3 conversion.
Additional measures include glucocorticoids (e.g., hydrocortisone), antibiotics if infection is present, cholestyramine, cooling, oxygen, and IV fluids.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.title: Treatment of Moderate-to-Severe Ophthalmopathy
items.content: Moderate-to-severe ophthalmopathy in Graves' disease requires treatment due to its significant impact on daily life. The preferred initial treatment is intravenous glucocorticoids, with teprotumumab as a recommended first-line therapy for active cases.
keyPoints: Mild ophthalmopathy usually improves spontaneously; general measures include controlling thyroid hormone levels and cessation of smoking.
Moderate-to-severe ophthalmopathy treated with IV methylprednisolone: 500 mg once weekly for 6 weeks, then 250 mg once weekly for 6 weeks.

Teprotumumab is a monoclonal antibody inhibitor of the IGF-1 receptor and improves proptosis, diplopia, clinical activity score, and quality of life; recommended as first-line treatment in active moderate-to-severe cases with significant symptoms.
Relapse rates for teprotumumab are similar to glucocorticoids (30%).

Sight-threatening ophthalmopathy requires immediate high-dose IV glucocorticoids (e.g., methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg on alternate days). Poor response after 2 weeks may necessitate surgical orbital decompression.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.title: Approach to Sight-Threatening Ophthalmopathy
items.content: Sight-threatening ophthalmopathy in Graves' disease is an emergency that requires immediate intervention with high-dose intravenous glucocorticoids. If there is no adequate response, surgical orbital decompression may be necessary.
keyPoints: Immediate treatment with high-dose IV glucocorticoids (e.g., methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg on alternate days) for optic nerve compression or corneal damage.
Poor response after 2 weeks to IV glucocorticoids indicates the need for surgical orbital decompression.
Surgical decompression involves removing bone from the medial and inferior orbital wall, allowing displacement of fat and swollen extraocular muscles.
Proptosis recedes an average of 5 mm post-surgery; however, there may be residual or worsened diplopia.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.subtopic: OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS
items.title: Distinguishing Destructive Thyroiditis from Other Causes of Low Radionuclide Uptake in Thyrotoxicosis
items.content: Destructive thyroiditis, including subacute or silent thyroiditis, presents with thyrotoxicosis due to the release of preformed thyroid hormones and catabolism of Tg. It is characterized by low radionuclide uptake and increased circulating Tg levels. Other causes of thyrotoxicosis with similarly low radionuclide uptake include thyrotoxicosis factitia, iodine excess, and rare ectopic thyroid tissue. Thyrotoxicosis factitia can be differentiated from destructive thyroiditis through clinical features and low Tg levels.
keyPoints: Destructive thyroiditis presents with low radionuclide uptake and increased circulating Tg levels.
Other causes of thyrotoxicosis with low radionuclide uptake include thyrotoxicosis factitia, iodine excess, and ectopic thyroid tissue.
Thyrotoxicosis factitia is distinguished from destructive thyroiditis by clinical features and low Tg levels.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.subtopic: OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Thyroiditis
items.content: Acute thyroiditis, a rare condition due to suppurative infection, presents with thyroid pain, fever, dysphagia, erythema over the thyroid, and systemic symptoms. It is often associated with risk factors like piriform sinus in young adults or degeneration in elderly patients with goiter or malignancy. Diagnosis involves increased ESR and white cell count, with FNA biopsy showing polymorphonuclear leukocytes. In immunocompromised patients, fungal, mycobacterial, or Pneumocystis infections should be considered. Treatment includes antibiotics guided by Gram stain and cultures, with possible surgical drainage for abscesses.
keyPoints: Acute thyroiditis presents with thyroid pain, fever, dysphagia, erythema over the thyroid, and systemic symptoms.
Risk factors include piriform sinus in young adults or degeneration in elderly patients with goiter or malignancy.
Diagnosis involves increased ESR and white cell count, with FNA biopsy showing polymorphonuclear leukocytes.
Treatment includes antibiotics guided by Gram stain and cultures, with possible surgical drainage for abscesses.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.title: Recognizing Subacute Thyroiditis and its Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: Subacute thyroiditis, also known as de Quervain's thyroiditis, can be mistaken for pharyngitis due to similar symptoms. Diagnosis is often confirmed by characteristic laboratory findings rather than identifying the causative virus.
keyPoints: Occurs with viruses like mumps, coxsackie, influenza, adenoviruses, and echoviruses; also associated with SARS-CoV-2 illness or COVID vaccine.
Peak incidence is 30–50 years, more common in women (3:1 ratio).
Diagnosis may be overlooked due to symptom overlap with pharyngitis.
Radioactive iodine uptake is low (<5%) during the destructive phase.
High ESR and negative thyroid antibodies help confirm diagnosis.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SUBACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.title: Understanding the Phases of Subacute Thyroiditis and Clinical Management
items.content: Subacute thyroiditis progresses through three phases: thyrotoxic, hypothyroid, and recovery. Each phase has distinct clinical and laboratory features that guide management.
keyPoints: Thyrotoxic phase: Increased T4 and T3, suppressed TSH; low T4/T3 ratio compared to Graves' disease.
Hypothyroid phase follows depletion of thyroid hormones with low unbound T4 (and sometimes T3) and moderately increased TSH levels.
Recovery phase sees normalization of thyroid hormone and TSH levels.
15% risk of late-onset permanent hypothyroidism, especially with concurrent thyroid autoimmunity.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SUBACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.title: Management of Subacute Thyroiditis: NSAIDs and Glucocorticoids
items.content: In subacute thyroiditis, initial management includes the use of aspirin or NSAIDs to control symptoms. If these are inadequate, glucocorticoids such as prednisone (15–40 mg) may be prescribed based on severity, with a taper over 6–8 weeks guided by symptom improvement and ESR levels.
keyPoints: Aspirin or NSAIDs can manage symptoms in many cases of subacute thyroiditis.
If inadequate, glucocorticoids (15–40 mg prednisone) are used based on severity.
Taper glucocorticoid dose over 6–8 weeks, guided by symptom improvement and ESR.
Monitor thyroid function every 2–4 weeks using TSH and free T₄ levels.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Subacute Thyroiditis | SILENT THYROIDITIS

items.title: Characteristics of Silent Thyroiditis: Clinical Course and Management
items.content: Silent thyroiditis, often postpartum, involves a brief thyrotoxic phase (2–4 weeks) followed by hypothyroidism (4–12 weeks), with spontaneous resolution. It is distinguished from subacute thyroiditis by normal ESR and presence of TPO antibodies. Management includes propranolol for severe thyrotoxic symptoms and potential LT4 replacement during the hypothyroid phase.
keyPoints: Occurs in up to 5% of women postpartum, often with a brief thyrotoxic phase (2–4 weeks).
Followed by hypothyroidism for 4–12 weeks, then resolution; may present only one phase.
Distinguished from subacute thyroiditis by normal ESR and presence of TPO antibodies.
Propranolol (20–40 mg) can manage severe thyrotoxic symptoms.
LT4 replacement may be needed for hypothyroid phase but should be withdrawn after 6–9 months.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Subacute Thyroiditis | SILENT THYROIDITIS

items.title: Drug-induced thyroiditis: Recognize potential triggers and monitor accordingly.
items.content: Certain medications, including cytokines like IFN-$\alpha$, tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as sorafenib, and immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab or nivolumab), can induce painless thyroiditis. The incidence of thyroid dysfunction in patients treated with IFN-$\alpha$ is up to 5%, often leading to conditions like hypothyroidism or Graves' disease, particularly in women with pre-existing TPO antibodies. For cancer patients on immune checkpoint inhibitors, the occurrence ranges from 5% to 20%. Routine monitoring of thyroid function tests is recommended.
keyPoints: IFN-$\alpha$ causes thyroid dysfunction in up to 5% of treated patients.
Thyroiditis occurs in 5–20% of cancer patients on pembrolizumab or nivolumab.
Routine monitoring of thyroid function tests is recommended by the American Society of Clinical Oncology.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · DRUG-INDUCED THYROIDITIS | CHRONIC THYROIDITIS

items.title: Riedel's thyroiditis: Identify and manage this rare fibrotic condition.
items.content: Riedel's thyroiditis is a rare disorder characterized by dense fibrosis of the thyroid gland, often presenting as an insidious, painless goiter. It primarily affects middle-aged women and can cause local symptoms due to compression of surrounding structures like the esophagus or trachea. Despite extensive histologic changes, thyroid dysfunction is uncommon. The firm, nontender, asymmetric goiter may mimic malignancy, necessitating open biopsy for diagnosis as FNA is usually inadequate. Treatment options include glucocorticoids, immunomodulatory treatments, tamoxifen, or surgery to relieve compressive symptoms.
keyPoints: Riedel's thyroiditis presents with a firm, nontender, often asymmetric goiter.
Thyroid dysfunction is uncommon despite extensive histologic changes.
Diagnosis requires open biopsy; FNA biopsy is usually inadequate.
Treatment options include glucocorticoids, immunomodulatory treatments, tamoxifen, or surgery.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · DRUG-INDUCED THYROIDITIS | CHRONIC THYROIDITIS

items.title: Understanding Hormonal Changes in Acute Illness
items.content: Acute, severe illnesses can cause misleading abnormalities in TSH or thyroid hormone levels due to cytokine release, particularly IL-6. Routine thyroid function tests should be avoided unless a thyroid disorder is strongly suspected.
keyPoints: Cytokines like IL-6 cause hormonal changes.
Avoid routine thyroid testing in acutely ill patients unless necessary.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.title: Characteristics of Low T3 Syndrome
items.content: In sick euthyroid syndrome, the most common pattern is decreased total and unbound T3 levels with normal T4 and TSH. This decrease correlates with illness severity due to impaired peripheral 5' deiodination.
keyPoints: Low T3 syndrome: Decreased total and unbound T3, normal T4 and TSH.
Impaired conversion of T4 to T3 via peripheral 5' deiodination.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.title: Interpreting Thyroid Function in Severe Illness
items.content: In very sick patients, T4 and T3 levels may fall dramatically. Altered binding to thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) can affect free T4 assay results. TSH levels fluctuate widely, complicating interpretation.
keyPoints: Low T4 syndrome: Dramatic fall in total T4 and T3.
Altered TBG affects free T4 assays.
TSH levels range from 0.01 to >20 mIU/L depending on illness phase.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.title: Amiodarone and Thyroid Function Suppression
items.content: Amiodarone, a type III antiarrhythmic agent, can cause acute, transient suppression of thyroid function due to its high iodine content (39% by weight) and inhibition of deiodinase activity.
keyPoints: Amiodarone contains 39% iodine by weight.
Typical doses lead to >40-fold increase in plasma and urinary iodine levels.
Inhibits $ T_{4} $ release, causing transient decrease in $ T_{4} $ levels.
High iodine levels persist for >6 months after discontinuation.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · AMIODARONE EFFECTS ON THYROID FUNCTION

items.title: Amiodarone-Induced Thyrotoxicosis Mechanisms
items.content: Amiodarone can induce thyrotoxicosis through mechanisms such as the Jod-Basedow effect, exacerbation of multinodular goiter (MNG) or incipient Graves' disease, and a thyroiditis-like condition.
keyPoints: Thyrotoxicosis may result from Jod-Basedow effect due to high iodine load.
Can exacerbate MNG or incipient Graves' disease.
May cause thyroiditis-like condition affecting thyroid follicular cells.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · AMIODARONE EFFECTS ON THYROID FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which population is most commonly affected by Graves' disease?
items.explanation: Graves' disease occurs in up to 2% of women but is one-tenth as frequent in men, typically between ages 20 and 50.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary cause of hyperthyroidism in Graves' disease?
options: Overproduction of TSH by the pituitary gland
items.explanation: The hyperthyroidism in Graves' disease is caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSIs).
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which factor is NOT associated with an increased risk for Graves' disease?
items.explanation: Low iodine intake is not mentioned as a risk factor; high iodine intake, smoking, and stress are associated with increased risk.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is a major clinical manifestation of thyroid-associated ophthalmopathy in Graves' disease?
options: Increased intra-orbital pressure leading to proptosis
Decreased intra-orbital pressure resulting in ptosis
Reduced glycosaminoglycan synthesis causing muscle atrophy
Enhanced insulin-like growth factor 1 receptor signaling
items.explanation: Increased intra-orbital pressure from glycosaminoglycans can lead to proptosis, diplopia, and optic neuropathy.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis | THYROTOXICOSIS | GRAVES' DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which cardiovascular manifestation is most common in Graves' disease?
items.explanation: The most common cardiovascular manifestation is sinus tachycardia.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients with Graves' disease experience diffuse alopecia?
items.explanation: Diffuse alopecia occurs in up to 40% of patients.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is the typical size increase of the thyroid gland in Graves' disease?
options: One to two times its normal size
Two to three times its normal size
Three to four times its normal size
Four to five times its normal size
items.explanation: The thyroid is usually diffusely enlarged to two to three times its normal size.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients with Graves' ophthalmopathy have unilateral signs?
items.explanation: Unilateral signs are found in up to 10% of ophthalmopathy patients.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a typical lesion associated with thyroid dermopathy in Graves' disease?
options: A noninflamed, indurated plaque with a deep pink or purple color and an 'orange skin' appearance
An inflamed, soft plaque with a pale yellow color
A nodular lesion that rapidly progresses to ulceration
A diffuse rash covering the entire lower leg
items.explanation: The typical lesion in thyroid dermopathy is described as noninflamed, indurated plaque with a deep pink or purple color and an 'orange skin' appearance.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: What percentage of patients with Graves' disease develop thyroid acropachy?
items.explanation: Thyroid acropachy occurs in less than 1% of patients with Graves' disease.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is typically suppressed in Graves' disease?
items.explanation: In Graves' disease, the TSH level is suppressed.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: Which diagnostic tool can distinguish between diffuse high uptake of Graves’ disease and other causes like destructive thyroiditis?
options: Radionuclide scan with ⁹⁹mTc, ¹²³I, or ¹³¹I
items.explanation: A radionuclide (⁹⁹mTc, ¹²³I, or ¹³¹I) scan and uptake of the thyroid can distinguish the diffuse high uptake of Graves’ disease from other causes.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · GRAVES' DISEASE (part 3)

items.question: Which antithyroid drug is limited by the FDA to use only in the first trimester of pregnancy, treatment of thyroid storm, and patients with minor adverse reactions to methimazole due to hepatotoxicity?
items.explanation: The FDA has limited the use of propylthiouracil to specific cases due to its hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 1)

items.question: What is a common initial dose regimen for methimazole or carbimazole in treating Graves' disease?
items.explanation: The initial dose of carbimazole or methimazole is usually 10–20 mg every 12 hours.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 1)

items.question: Which antithyroid drug regimen involves combining high doses with levothyroxine supplementation to avoid hypothyroidism?
items.explanation: The block-replace regimen involves high doses of antithyroid drugs combined with levothyroxine supplementation.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 1)

items.question: What is the usual duration before most patients achieve euthyroidism after starting treatment for Graves' disease?
items.explanation: Most patients do not achieve euthyroidism until 6–8 weeks after treatment is initiated.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 1)

items.question: Which medication is recommended for controlling adrenergic symptoms in the early stages of Graves' disease before antithyroid drugs take effect?
items.explanation: Propranolol is recommended to control adrenergic symptoms in the early stages of Graves' disease.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.question: What should be done with antithyroid drugs before administering radioiodine treatment?
options: Stop them 2–3 days before and restart 3–7 days after
Increase the dose immediately before administration
items.explanation: Carbimazole or methimazole must be stopped 2–3 days before radioiodine to achieve optimum iodine uptake.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.question: What is the general range of $^{131}$I dosage used in treating Graves' disease with radioiodine?
items.explanation: The general range of $^{131}$I dosage used is between 370 MBq (10 mCi) and 555 MBq (15 mCi).
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.question: What precaution should be taken after radioiodine treatment to prevent radiation exposure to others?
options: Avoid close contact with children and pregnant women for 5–7 days
Wear a lead apron at all times
items.explanation: Patients should avoid close, prolonged contact with children and pregnant women for 5–7 days after treatment.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.question: Which medication is recommended for use until 14–16 weeks of gestation in pregnant women with Graves' disease to minimize the risk of embryopathy?
items.explanation: Propylthiouracil is preferred until 14–16 weeks' gestation due to the risk of methimazole/carbimazole embryopathy.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 3)

items.question: What is a major complication that can occur after thyroidectomy for Graves' disease?
items.explanation: Major complications of surgery include bleeding, laryngeal edema, hypoparathyroidism, and damage to the recurrent laryngeal nerves.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 3)

items.question: Which treatment is often discontinued in the last trimester of pregnancy for women with Graves' disease?
items.explanation: It is often possible to stop antithyroid drug treatment in the last trimester because TSI tend to decline.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is the preferred initial treatment for thyrotoxic crisis due to its inhibitory action on T4 to T3 conversion?
items.explanation: Propylthiouracil is preferred due to its inhibitory action on T4 to T3 conversion.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.question: What is the initial dose of propylthiouracil recommended for managing thyrotoxic crisis?
options: 500-1000 mg loading dose and 250 mg every 4 hours
items.explanation: The recommended initial dose is a 500–1000 mg loading dose followed by 250 mg every 4 hours.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.question: Which treatment should be administered one hour after the first dose of propylthiouracil or methimazole to block thyroid hormone synthesis?
items.explanation: Stable iodide (SSKI) is given one hour after the first dose to block thyroid hormone synthesis.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for moderate-to-severe ophthalmopathy in Graves' disease?
items.explanation: IV methylprednisolone is recommended for moderate-to-severe ophthalmopathy.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.subtopic: OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS
items.question: Which condition is characterized by an inappropriately normal or increased TSH level, diffuse goiter, and elevated $T_4$ and $T_3$ levels?
items.explanation: A TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma is characterized by these specific hormonal and clinical features.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.subtopic: OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS
items.question: Which of the following can be distinguished from destructive thyroiditis by low levels of Tg?
items.explanation: Thyrotoxicosis factitia can be distinguished from destructive thyroiditis by low levels of Tg.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.subtopic: OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS
items.question: What is a common cause of acute thyroiditis in children and young adults?
items.explanation: A piriform sinus, a remnant of the fourth branchial pouch, is a common cause in children and young adults.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · OTHER CAUSES OF THYROTOXICOSIS | THYROIDITIS | ACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.question: Which of the following viruses has NOT been implicated in subacute thyroiditis?
items.explanation: The passage lists mumps, coxsackie, influenza, adenoviruses, and echoviruses as implicated viruses in subacute thyroiditis.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SUBACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.question: During which phase of subacute thyroiditis is radioactive iodine uptake low or undetectable?
items.explanation: In the initial destructive phase, which corresponds to the thyrotoxic phase, radioactive iodine uptake is low or undetectable.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SUBACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.question: What percentage of patients with subacute thyroiditis may develop late-onset permanent hypothyroidism?
items.explanation: Late-onset permanent hypothyroidism occurs in 15% of cases, particularly in those with coincidental thyroid autoimmunity.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SUBACUTE THYROIDITIS

items.question: Which medication is typically used to manage severe thyrotoxic symptoms in silent thyroiditis?
items.explanation: Severe thyrotoxic symptoms in silent thyroiditis can be managed with a brief course of propranolol.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Subacute Thyroiditis | SILENT THYROIDITIS

items.question: What is the usual initial treatment for subacute thyroiditis?
items.explanation: Relatively large doses of aspirin or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are sufficient to control symptoms in many cases of subacute thyroiditis.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Subacute Thyroiditis | SILENT THYROIDITIS

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature between silent thyroiditis and subacute thyroiditis?
items.explanation: Silent thyroiditis can be distinguished from subacute thyroiditis by a normal ESR and the presence of TPO antibodies.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Subacute Thyroiditis | SILENT THYROIDITIS

items.question: Which cytokine is associated with thyroid dysfunction in up to 5% of treated patients?
items.explanation: IFN-α is used for chronic hepatitis B or C and hematologic malignancies, causing thyroid dysfunction in up to 5% of patients.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · DRUG-INDUCED THYROIDITIS | CHRONIC THYROIDITIS

items.question: What percentage of cancer patients treated with pembrolizumab or nivolumab develop thyroiditis?
items.explanation: Thyroiditis occurs in 5–20% of cancer patients treated with pembrolizumab or nivolumab.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · DRUG-INDUCED THYROIDITIS | CHRONIC THYROIDITIS

items.question: Which treatment is recommended for thyroiditis caused by immune checkpoint inhibitors?
items.explanation: Treatment of thyroiditis from immune checkpoint inhibitors is the same as for silent thyroiditis.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · DRUG-INDUCED THYROIDITIS | CHRONIC THYROIDITIS

items.question: Which cytokine is primarily responsible for the hormonal changes seen in sick euthyroid syndrome?
items.explanation: The major cause of hormonal changes in sick euthyroid syndrome is the release of cytokines such as IL-6.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.question: What is the most common hormone pattern observed in sick euthyroid syndrome?
options: Decreased T3 with normal T4 and TSH
items.explanation: The most common hormone pattern in sick euthyroid syndrome is a decrease in total and unbound T3 levels (low T3 syndrome) with normal levels of T4 and TSH.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.question: What happens to reverse T3 (rT3) levels in sick euthyroid syndrome?
options: Increased conversion of T4 to rT3
items.explanation: In sick euthyroid syndrome, decreased clearance rather than increased production is the major basis for increased rT3.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.question: Which condition can be associated with an initial rise in total T3 and T4 levels due to TBG release?
items.explanation: Acute liver disease is associated with an initial rise in total (but not unbound) T3 and T4 levels due to TBG release.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.question: Which of the following is a mechanism by which amiodarone affects thyroid function?
options: Stimulation of $ T_{4} $ to $ T_{3} $ conversion
Reduction in plasma and urinary iodine levels
items.explanation: Amiodarone inhibits deiodinase activity, affecting thyroid function.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · AMIODARONE EFFECTS ON THYROID FUNCTION

items.question: What is a potential consequence of the high iodine load from amiodarone in susceptible individuals?
items.explanation: Amiodarone can cause hypothyroidism in patients susceptible to high iodine load.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · AMIODARONE EFFECTS ON THYROID FUNCTION

items.question: Which condition may result from amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis?
items.explanation: Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis may result from a Jod-Basedow effect.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · AMIODARONE EFFECTS ON THYROID FUNCTION

items.statement: TSH levels are a sensitive index of treatment response immediately after starting antithyroid drugs.

items.statement: Remission rates for Graves' disease are higher in patients with persistent TRAb levels.

items.statement: Postpartum period poses no significant risk of relapse in Graves' disease.

items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 3)

items.statement: Teprotumumab, a monoclonal antibody inhibitor of the IGF-1 receptor, is recommended as first-line treatment for active moderate-to-severe ophthalmopathy with significant proptosis or diplopia.

items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.statement: Thyroid dermopathy usually requires surgical intervention.
items.explanation: Thyroid dermopathy does not usually require treatment, and surgical removal is not indicated.

items.statement: Riedel's thyroiditis commonly leads to thyroid dysfunction.

items.statement: Focal thyroiditis is associated with serologic evidence of autoimmunity.

items.assertion: Graves' disease can be precipitated by sudden increases in iodine intake.
items.reason: Sudden increases in iodine intake may lead to a threefold increase in the occurrence of Graves' disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.reason: TRAb assays measure whether the patient's serum contains an antibody that can displace labeled TSH or a monoclonal TSH receptor antibody from the TSH receptor.

items.assertion: The thyroid gland in Graves' disease is nodular.
items.reason: The consistency of the thyroid gland is firm but not nodular.

items.assertion: Ophthalmopathy in Graves' disease typically worsens over the initial 3–6 months.

items.assertion: Radioiodine treatment for hyperthyroidism has no adverse effects on the clinical course of ophthalmopathy.

items.assertion: Propylthiouracil is preferred over methimazole for long-term treatment due to its shorter half-life.
items.reason: Propylthiouracil has a much shorter half-life compared to methimazole.
items.explanation: The shorter half-life of propylthiouracil is an offset, not a reason for preference in long-term treatment.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 1)

items.assertion: Younger patients and smokers are more likely to relapse after stopping antithyroid drug treatment.
items.reason: These groups have higher remission rates during initial treatment.
items.explanation: While younger patients and smokers are more likely to relapse, the reason is not due to higher remission rates.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 1)

items.assertion: Radioiodine therapy can lead to hypothyroidism.
items.reason: The risk of hypothyroidism is at least 10–20% in the first year after treatment and increases over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.assertion: Prednisone is used to treat hyperthyroidism directly.
items.reason: Prednisone is administered at the time of radioiodine treatment to prevent exacerbation of ophthalmopathy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because prednisone is not used to treat hyperthyroidism directly.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 2)

items.assertion: Propranolol is used in thyrotoxic crisis to reduce tachycardia.
items.reason: High doses of propranolol decrease T4 to T3 conversion, which helps manage symptoms.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason given does not directly explain why propranolol reduces tachycardia.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.assertion: Sight-threatening ophthalmopathy requires immediate high-dose IV glucocorticoids.
items.reason: This treatment is necessary to prevent optic nerve compression or corneal damage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the necessity of the treatment.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · Graves' Disease (part 4)

items.assertion: The low T3 state in sick euthyroid syndrome is considered adaptive.
items.reason: It can be induced in normal individuals by fasting and may limit catabolism in starved or ill patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the low T3 state is considered adaptive.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.assertion: In renal disease, rT3 levels are increased due to decreased uptake into the liver.
items.reason: Renal disease is often accompanied by low T3 concentrations with normal rather than increased rT3 levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because in renal disease, rT3 levels are not increased; they remain normal due to an unknown factor that increases uptake into the liver.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · SICK EUTHYROID SYNDROME (NONTHYROIDAL ILLNESS)

items.assertion: Amiodarone can cause hypothyroidism in susceptible individuals.
items.reason: This is due to the inhibitory effects of a high iodine load from amiodarone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis · AMIODARONE EFFECTS ON THYROID FUNCTION
